startingHypothesis.statement: Trust in AI systems is becoming an evidence problem: durable trust will depend less on stated intent and more on independently repeatable assurance signals across evaluation, monitoring, and operational control.
startingHypothesis.rationale: Multiple fresh sources point toward operational evidence, evaluation, and monitoring as core trust mechanisms.

sourcesRead.title: Auditing Smart and Sustainable Supply Chains: The Role of Assurance in AI-Driven Decision Systems
sourcesRead.summary: Artificial intelligence, big data analytics, blockchain-enabled traceability, smart contracts, platform-based coordination, robotic process automation, and real-time digital systems are reshaping the governance architecture of contemporary supply chains. In smart and sustainable supply chain environments, managerial decisions concerning procurement, supplier evaluation, logistics optimization, corporate social responsibility reporting, sustainability reporting, carbon estimation, and risk monitoring are mediated by

sourcesRead.title: From tool to collaborator: rethinking GIScience as GIS becomes agentic and autonomous
sourcesRead.summary: Geographic Information Systems (GIS) have traditionally been positioned along Wright, Goodchild and Proctor’s continuum of GIS as tool, toolmaking, and GIScience, a framework that treats GIS as primarily executing human-authored intent. This paper argues that agentic and autonomous GIS strains that assumption by moving GIS beyond predefined command execution toward intent interpretation, workflow construction, and iterative procedural revision.

notes.claim: Auditing Smart and Sustainable Supply Chains: The Role of Assurance in AI-Driven Decision Systems adds evidence on assurance measurement.
notes.implication: The trust question should be treated as a testable evidence pipeline, not a static policy claim.
notes.limitation: The metadata-only pass should be followed by closer reading before major confidence changes.

synthesis: Tonight's open-source scan found 2 relevant artifacts clustered around smart, supply, sustainable, chains. The working hypothesis remains viable, but the evidence base is still stronger on evaluation and tooling than on independent, repeatable assurance in deployed systems.

hypothesisUpdates.rationale: Multiple fresh sources point toward operational evidence, evaluation, and monitoring as core trust mechanisms.

newHypotheses.statement: Independent AI assurance will converge around continuously refreshed evidence bundles rather than one-time model reports.
Agentic AI trust failures will be easier to detect through workflow telemetry than through benchmark scores alone.

nextSearchPlan: continuous assurance evidence bundles AI systems
agent workflow telemetry trust monitoring evaluation
independent AI audit benchmark operational assurance
unresolvedQuestions: Which assurance signals remain predictive after deployment conditions drift?
What evidence should be independently reproducible versus vendor-attested?
How should agentic workflows expose trust-relevant telemetry without leaking sensitive data?
